claims.claim: Sijil Kemahiran Malaysia Holders Earn 40% Less Than Degree Holders
claims.source: No primary source found for the wage comparison. Arithmetic only: RM2,100 is 40% below RM3,500 if those inputs were correct.
claims.correction: Do not publish as a headline unless a primary wage dataset or named survey methodology verifies the RM2,100 and RM3,500 comparison by qualification and same field.
claims.stale_check: Checked on 2026-05-17; no evidence found that this became verifiable through a recent official release.

claims.claim: Skills certificate holders face persistent wage discrimination despite equivalent competency in technical fields.
claims.source: Department of Skills Development, Malaysian Skills Certificate page, crawled 2026-05, https://www.dsd.gov.my/en/service/malaysian-skills-certificate-skm; MQA MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf
claims.correction: The analysis needs primary evidence for discrimination and for the claim of equivalent competency. Skills certification verifies occupational competencies, but the checked MQF and DSD documents do not establish that SKM holders and degree holders have equivalent competency in the same technical fields.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that would validate this broad causal claim.

claims.claim: SKM Level 4 holders earn average RM2,100 versus RM3,500 for degree holders in same fields
claims.source: No primary source found. Searches for the exact RM2,100/RM3,500 SKM-degree comparison did not locate an official dataset, regulator report, court record, peer-reviewed paper, or primary survey.
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced official wage statistic, or label clearly as an unverified estimate. Also clarify whether the credential is SKM Level 3, DKM Level 4, or another qualification.
claims.stale_check: Checked on 2026-05-17; no recent primary source found.

claims.claim: Wage gap persists even when job functions are identical between qualifications
claims.source: No primary source found verifying identical job-function wage comparisons for SKM/DKM and degree holders.
claims.correction: This requires job-matched wage data or a study controlling for role, sector, experience, location, and employer type.

claims.claim: Malaysian Qualifications Framework places SKM and degrees at equivalent levels since 2007
claims.source: MQA, Malaysian Qualifications Framework Second Edition 2024, lines showing first published 2007 and implementation enforced 2011, and Level 4 Diploma/Level 6 Bachelor’s Degree, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf; Department of Skills Development, Malaysian Skills Certificate page, https://www.dsd.gov.my/en/service/malaysian-skills-certificate-skm
claims.correction: MQF was first published in 2007 and implementation was enforced in 2011. DSD lists SKM Levels 1-3, Malaysian Skills Diploma (DKM) Level 4, and Malaysian Advanced Skills Diploma (DLKM) Level 5. MQF places diploma at Level 4 and bachelor’s degree at Level 6, so SKM/DKM and degrees are not generally at equivalent MQF levels.
claims.stale_check: Current MQA page last updated 2025-11-19 and last modified 2026-05-11; the 2024 MQF confirms the correction.

claims.claim: Formal equivalence has not translated to employer recognition or pay parity
claims.correction: The premise of formal equivalence between SKM/DKM and degrees is not supported. MQF does promote parity of esteem among academic, professional, technical, vocational and skills training providers, but this is not the same as pay parity or equivalence to degrees.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found supporting the equivalence premise.

claims.claim: 74% of employers in MIHRM 2025 survey require degrees for jobs performable by SKM holders
claims.source: No primary MIHRM 2025 survey found. Searches for MIHRM, 74%, degree requirements, SKM, and employer survey did not locate an official MIHRM report or methodology.
claims.correction: Remove unless the original MIHRM survey report, sample size, field dates, questionnaire wording, and definition of jobs performable by SKM holders can be produced.
claims.stale_check: Checked on 2026-05-17; no evidence found that this is a recently released verifiable survey.

claims.claim: Credentialism rather than competency drives hiring practices
claims.source: No primary source found tying Malaysian employer hiring practices for SKM/DKM roles to credentialism rather than competency.
claims.correction: Frame as a hypothesis or quote a verifiable primary employer survey. The current wording asserts motive and causality without evidence.

claims.claim: Switzerland's vocational diploma holders earn 92% of university graduate salaries on average
claims.source: Swiss Federal Statistical Office, income development of higher vocational education graduates, January 2023, https://www.swissstats.bfs.admin.ch/data/webviewer/appId/ch.admin.bfs.swissstat/article/issue231522272300-01/package; economiesuisse article citing federal graduate survey, 2020, https://www.economiesuisse.ch/de/artikel/erfolgreicher-berufseinstieg-fuer-hochschulabsolventen
claims.correction: The apparent 92% figure matches economiesuisse’s comparison of universities of applied sciences bachelor graduates CHF87,200 versus university master graduates CHF95,000 five years after graduation, not vocational diploma holders generally. The primary FSO higher vocational education data gives different figures: six years after higher vocational education, median full-time monthly income is about CHF7,800 overall, CHF10,100 for federal diploma, CHF7,800 for federal certificate, and CHF7,500 for higher technical college diploma.
claims.stale_check: The checked FSO source is from January 2023; no newer primary source was found that confirms the exact 92% vocational-diploma wording.

claims.claim: Strong industry-certification linkage ensures skills-based pay not credential-based
claims.source: No primary source found establishing this causal mechanism for Switzerland or Malaysia.
claims.correction: Needs evidence from Swiss federal education/labour authorities or peer-reviewed labour-market research. Current wording overstates causality.

claims.claim: Devaluing skills certificates while promoting TVET sends contradictory signals to young people
claims.source: MQA MQF Second Edition 2024 notes Eleventh and Twelfth Malaysia Plan directives for flexible learning pathways and a stronger quality TVET workforce, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf
claims.correction: Government promotion of TVET is supported in broad policy terms, but devaluing skills certificates and the effect on young people are not verified by primary evidence here.
claims.stale_check: No recent primary evidence found for the devaluation/effect claim.

claims.claim: Should government mandate equal pay for equivalent MQF-level qualifications in public sector
claims.source: MQA MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf; Department of Skills Development, Malaysian Skills Certificate page, https://www.dsd.gov.my/en/service/malaysian-skills-certificate-skm
claims.correction: As a policy question it is not directly true or false, but the factual premise is problematic: SKM/DKM and degrees are not generally equivalent MQF-level qualifications. If discussing public-sector pay, cite the specific service circulars and grade schemes.
claims.stale_check: No recent source found changing MQF level placement.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: SKM Level 4 holders earn average RM2,100 versus RM3,500 for degree holders in same fields
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Specific wage numbers, qualification category, and same-field comparison could not be traced to a primary source.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysian Qualifications Framework places SKM and degrees at equivalent levels since 2007
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official MQA and DSD sources contradict the claim. DSD lists SKM Levels 1-3, DKM Level 4, DLKM Level 5; MQF lists Diploma at Level 4 and Bachelor’s Degree at Level 6.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: MQA MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf; DSD SKM page, https://www.dsd.gov.my/en/service/malaysian-skills-certificate-skm

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 74% of employers in MIHRM 2025 survey require degrees for jobs performable by SKM holders
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No official MIHRM 2025 survey or primary methodology was found for this specific 74% claim.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Switzerland's vocational diploma holders earn 92% of university graduate salaries on average
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The 92% comparison appears to come from a secondary article comparing universities of applied sciences bachelor graduates with university master graduates, not vocational diploma holders generally. Primary FSO vocational data shows multiple different income figures by higher vocational credential.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Skills certificate holders face persistent wage discrimination despite equivalent competency in technical fields.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analysis asserts discrimination, persistence, and equivalent competency without primary evidence. Official qualification frameworks do not establish equivalent competency between SKM/DKM and degrees across technical fields.

omitted_facts: MQA states the MQF was first published in 2007, implementation was enforced in 2011, the second edition was approved in December 2017, and a 2024 edition exists. Source: MQA MQF page and MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/mqf.cfm and https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf

DSD lists Malaysian Skills Certification as SKM Level 1, SKM Level 2, SKM Level 3, Malaysian Skills Diploma (DKM) Level 4, and Malaysian Advanced Skills Diploma (DLKM) Level 5. Source: Department of Skills Development, https://www.dsd.gov.my/en/service/malaysian-skills-certificate-skm

MQF 2024 lists Level 4 as Diploma and Level 6 as Bachelor’s Degree; this directly undercuts the claim that SKM/DKM and degrees are equivalent levels. Source: MQA MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf

MASCO 2020 classifies tertiary education not equivalent to a first degree, SKM Level 3, or DKM Level 4 under Skill Level Third; university degree/postgraduate degree or DKM Level 5 and above under Skill Level Fourth. Source: Ministry of Human Resources MASCO 2020, https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2022-12/MASCO_2020_BI_Edaran.pdf

MQF’s stated objectives include encouraging parity of esteem among academic, professional, technical, vocational and skills training providers, but this is not an equal-pay rule. Source: MQA MQF Second Edition 2024, https://www.mqa.gov.my/new/document/2024/new/MQF%20%282024%29.pdf
source_assessment: The submitted analysis cites zero sources. It contains precise wage figures, a named 2025 MIHRM survey, and an international comparison, but none are backed by primary links or methodology. The only verifiable sources found independently were official MQA, DSD, MOHR/MASCO, and Swiss FSO materials, and these mostly contradict or complicate the claims. No employer, worker, union, public-sector pay circular, peer-reviewed Malaysian wage study, or MIHRM primary survey is represented.
overall_assessment: The analysis is factually unreliable in its current form. Its central claim that SKM/DKM qualifications and degrees are equivalent MQF levels is contradicted by official sources, while the key RM2,100/RM3,500 wage comparison and MIHRM 74% employer survey could not be verified. The Swiss comparison appears misframed. The piece should not be published without replacing the numeric claims with primary-sourced evidence and correcting the MQF qualification hierarchy.
